The slide likely suggests expressing 'study the apple on the towel' using a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) by breaking down the sentence into its constituent parts and assigning probabilities to each production rule. This involves creating a parse tree where each node represents a grammatical structure, such as a noun phrase (NP) or prepositional phrase (PP), and each branch represents a probabilistic rule that generates the structure. 1. S -> VP (Verb Phrase)
2. VP -> V NP PP (Verb, Noun Phrase, Prepositional Phrase)
3. V -> 'study'
4. NP -> Det N (Determiner, Noun)
5. Det -> 'the'
6. N -> 'apple'
7. PP -> P NP (Preposition, Noun Phrase)
8. P -> 'on'
9. NP -> Det N
10. Det -> 'the'
11. N -> 'towel'

Each rule would have an associated probability based on its likelihood in the language model.
